title: How Machines Learn Patterns From Data

lesson.learn:
Machine learning works by training a model on examples so it can find patterns and make predictions. Instead of hard-coding every rule, engineers provide data and let the system adjust its internal parameters until its outputs improve. That process is what lets machine learning handle vision, language, recommendations, and many other tasks.

The key idea is generalization. A good model does not just memorize examples. It learns a pattern that still works when new data arrives.
lesson.simpler: Machine learning teaches a computer by showing it examples. The computer gets better by spotting patterns in data.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Machine learning works when a model learns patterns from data that still hold on new examples.

learnImageCredit.creator: Hutchins BI, Davis MT, Meseroll RA, Santangelo GM.

lesson.deeperDive:
Most machine-learning systems use a loss function to measure error, then adjust parameters through optimization such as gradient descent. Supervised learning uses labeled examples, unsupervised learning finds structure without labels, and reinforcement learning learns from rewards. Deep learning stacks many layers so the model can represent very complex patterns.

That is why machine learning can be powerful and fragile at the same time. It can outperform hand-written rules in many tasks, but it can also overfit, drift, or fail when the world changes in ways the training data did not prepare it for.
lesson.funFact: A model can look brilliant on training data and still fail in the real world.

people: Andrew Ng - major machine learning educator
Fei-Fei Li - influential AI and vision researcher

years: 2012 - deep learning breaks through in vision
concepts: Training data - examples used to teach a model
Loss function - a measure of error
Gradient descent - a method for reducing error
Generalization - performing well on new data
